skill.name: calc
skill.description: Create, edit, convert, and automate spreadsheets with LibreOffice Calc, ODS files, formulas, and command-line workflows.
skill.summary: Automate LibreOffice Calc spreadsheet creation, conversion, formulas, and reporting workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown code fences as shell execution and several documentation links as network activity. Review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, secret access, or data exfiltration, but the skill legitimately guides agents to run LibreOffice, install Python packages, and read or write local spreadsheet files.

low_findings.title: Legitimate External Command Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill documents use of LibreOffice command-line tools and package installation commands. These are expected for spreadsheet automation, but users should review file paths and package sources before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown examples and match the stated LibreOffice Calc automation purpose. I found no evidence that the skill executes them automatically or hides user input in shell strings.

low_findings.title: Local LibreOffice Socket Usage
low_findings.description: The UNO examples connect to a LibreOffice service on localhost and include a command to start that local socket. This is normal for UNO automation but can expose a local control interface if used carelessly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The host is explicitly localhost, which limits exposure compared with a remote bind. The concern is operational hardening, not evidence of malicious network behavior.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Only
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to LibreOffice, the UNO API, and ezodf documentation. They do not receive local data or credentials in the skill text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only as resource links in the documentation section. I found no evidence of HTTP requests, tracking endpoints, credential transfer, or hidden exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Scanner False Positive: Markdown Backticks
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner classified Markdown code fences as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation delimiters, not executable Ruby syntax.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Each flagged backtick sequence is part of a fenced Markdown example in SKILL.md. No Ruby file or executable skill script exists in the provided file structure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Scanner False Positive: Network Reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: The flagged lines are LibreOffice conversion commands, localhost UNO socket examples, or related skill names. They are not network scanning commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain soffice conversion examples, a localhost socket for LibreOffice automation, and documentation labels. I found no evidence of port scanning, host discovery, or remote probing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Scanner False Positive: Weak Cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: The line flagged for weak cryptography is the YAML description listing spreadsheet formats such as ODS, XLSX, and CSV. It does not contain cryptographic code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain metadata text describing spreadsheet formats. I found no hashing, encryption, signature, or password-handling logic in the skill file.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet conversion and reporting often require repeated manual work across ODS, XLSX, CSV, and PDF files. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical LibreOffice Calc workflows for creation, conversion, formulas, and automation.
actual_capabilities: Create ODS spreadsheets with LibreOffice Calc workflows.
Convert between ODS, XLSX, CSV, PDF, HTML, XLS, and DBF formats.
Use formulas, named ranges, validation, filtering, and conditional formatting guidance.
Automate Calc through headless soffice commands and Python UNO examples.
Reference Python libraries such as ezodf, odfpy, and pandas for spreadsheet tasks.
Troubleshoot local LibreOffice socket issues for automation workflows.
limitations: Requires LibreOffice and optional Python packages to be installed locally.
Does not include executable helper scripts or packaged templates.
UNO automation examples assume a local LibreOffice listener on port 8100.
Conversion fidelity depends on LibreOffice support for each input file.

use_cases.title: Convert spreadsheet batches
use_cases.description: Convert a folder of ODS workbooks to XLSX or PDF using headless LibreOffice commands.

use_cases.title: Build formula reports
use_cases.description: Create Calc workbooks with formulas, summaries, and validation for recurring reports.

use_cases.title: Prototype spreadsheet automation
use_cases.description: Use UNO and Python library examples to design local spreadsheet automation workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a spreadsheet
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an ODS spreadsheet for a monthly expense tracker with category totals and a summary sheet.

prompt_templates.title: Convert file formats
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the LibreOffice Calc workflow to convert these ODS files to XLSX and PDF safely.

prompt_templates.title: Add formulas and validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Calc workbook with formulas, named ranges, input validation, and conditional formatting for sales data.

prompt_templates.title: Automate with UNO
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Python UNO workflow that opens a local Calc document, writes data, inserts formulas, and saves an ODS file.

output_examples.input: Create a budget workbook in ODS format.
output_examples.output: A workbook plan with income, expense, category summary, and monthly total sheets.

output_examples.input: Convert several ODS files to PDF.
output_examples.output: A safe headless LibreOffice conversion workflow with quoted file paths and verification steps.
A structured approach for adding values, formulas, named ranges, and validation through LibreOffice automation.

best_practices: Review every command and file path before running local automation.
Keep original ODS files under version control before conversion.
Test conversions with sample files before processing a large batch.
anti_patterns: Do not run conversion commands on untrusted spreadsheets without isolation.
Do not expose the LibreOffice UNO listener beyond localhost.
Do not install Python packages without checking the environment and source.

faq.question: Does this skill require LibreOffice?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflows rely on LibreOffice Calc and the soffice command-line tool.

faq.question: Can it convert XLSX files to ODS?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents LibreOffice conversions from XLSX and other formats to ODS.

faq.question: Does it include runnable scripts?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and examples rather than packaged automation scripts.

faq.question: Is network access required?
faq.answer: No remote network access is required for core workflows. Documentation links are included for reference.

faq.question: Can it create formulas?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for formulas, named ranges, validation, filtering, and automation.

faq.question: What should users review before running commands?
faq.answer: Users should review file paths, input files, package installs, and any local UNO socket configuration.
